title: Manufacturing 1 million drones a year | Soren Monroe-Anderson, Neros

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: On first pass we've defeated every single jammer that the US military has put us up against, so we played a bit of a prank on the soldiers. They knew our frequencies we were operating on, we'd already done a bunch of testing runs and they collected data. We flew seven kilometers over to the jammer and then just hovered in front of them for like 10 minutes. After 10 minutes with five jammers going at once, they got to taking out one of our control links, we have two. And we asked them on the radio like 'hey can you guys see the payload?' and they're like 'yep, we see you have a payload' and we're like 'no, can you see what the payload is?' we strapped a jar of Smucker's strawberry jam to our drone as the payload and then the soldier saw the Smucker's jam, the jammer has been defeated by the jam. In a real-world scenario we would just have come in and hit the target.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Today I have the pleasure of sitting down with Soren Monroe-Anderson, the co-founder and CEO of Neuros. Neuros is, I think, today the biggest FPV drone maker in America. When I interviewed you last, I think you were just moving into your old warehouse, which has like a max capacity of 10, 20, 30,000 drones a year, and we are now in your new 250,000 square foot warehouse with a max capacity of a million drones per year. Do you want to just start with like what was the ramp like at that old warehouse and what are you going to be able to do here?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so we moved into our old facility in March of 2024. We'd been in a very small garage before that. We had built like a couple hundred drones out of that little garage, but we knew ramping production capacity was kind of the number one goal of the company at that point. And we wanted to be able to, we didn't have the orders for lots of drones, but we wanted to be able to say like, 'Look, we can do it.' It took us about six months after moving into that facility to really have like a real assembly line going that we could kind of credibly say like, 'Give us the order, we'll start delivering.' But it wasn't until early 2025 that we were really shipping more than a few hundred drones per month. So it's honestly been a really fast ramp up over the last 12 months, and also just to fill that facility, not just from the production side, but the team size. You know, we were, Neuros was 16 people when 2025 started, and we are currently about 125, and I'd guess that number's going to be even higher just by the time this interview comes out. We're growing the team very rapidly.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Almost 10x a year.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: At this rate, yeah. So, you know, we thought, we only had like a, I think, three-year lease on the old building, so it's not like we were planning to be in there for a really long time, but we did, you know, we've really pushed the old facility to its limits now with both production and engineering. And so when we were, we started looking for a new building actually almost a year ago now because we were very...

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: picky on certain things. So we wanted to start the process early and we went through a few different phases of like what size of building we were going to get and, you know, we ended up landing on this 250,000 because we over the past 12 months have really oriented the company around being the first American drone company that can build a million drones per year. So we wanted to spec a facility that could support that goal and not just assembly and test, but component manufacturing. We've been with our current production running into limitations on what we can get from suppliers outside of China and we've sort of known for a long time that to do what we want to do eventually, it will really involve vertical integration. But with the demand signal we're seeing right now from the US government and from our allied partners and just the, honestly, just the direction that the world is going and how important FPV systems are, building massive sovereign capacity is really our number one mandate. So we wanted to find a size facility that could support the long-term growth of the company, not be another, you know, two, three year stepping stone, be something that we can actually build into the global headquarters of Nearos. You know, it'll be a lot of construction even to just get the like office space that we need in here. And then, you know, an empty building doesn't equal a million drones per year. There's huge CapEx investments and build out and just great, you know, engineering that needs to be done to, to actually turn this into a factory. So it's, you know, we haven't done the thing, but we've, we've bought ourselves a canvas to do the thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Basically have all the paints and the canvas ready to go and now you just got to go paint something.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I'd say the paint is still, you know, some of that's still lacking with talent that we need to hire. Um, you know, we have an amazing team and we have the right building blocks, but we gotta get a lot more great people in to, to be able to do to do this.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Very few people have kind of had a pulse like you have on how the Russia-Ukraine conflict has evolved. So do you want to just talk about what it was like going into early 2022 and how what's happened over the past three and a half years?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, coming up on, I mean, basically four years now. Uh, yeah, you know, Nearos has not been around for, for all of that, but I've been closely tracking since the full-scale invasion started. And when it started, it was a very impactful moment for me and I've talked about this in the past, so I won't go totally deep into that. But, um, I, it's been very, very interesting to see how it's made the world react to the technology that Nearos is building because, you know, we sort of had our own ideas even before the

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: We saw the Ukrainians doing it, and before Neuros was a company, we had our own ideas of drone racing technology applied to defense applications and, you know, thought it could be meaningful, but couldn't prove it really. And then the Ukrainians went and proved it. And then in 2023, when we were starting the company, we were just focused on building a better product for the Ukrainians that we were working with. And the excitement from the U.S. side was really limited.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: There was like, most most people in the Pentagon did not care about small quadcopters. Kind of understandably, because they had not been a meaningful capability increase, for the most part, from what they had seen. So focusing on Ukraine for the first, you know, 12 months of the company was, I think, the right decision because we ended up making a much more useful product out of that. And it's still a really big focus of the company. But it's been interesting to see that, you know, the mindset in America has totally flipped 180. Small drones are now a top priority from, you know, the senior leaders of the Pentagon and of the country.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Like a competency incredibly fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Exactly. We built up a competency, and then we were ready to deliver for these initial programs that the U.S. is spinning up. And now we are trying to build the capacity ahead of the government actually putting out contracts for, you know, millions of drones per year. There's been some talk from the Pentagon and from the Army saying that they're going to try to acquire a million drones over the next year or two. Nobody exists to deliver that right now. And I think, you know, that's not going to happen on a one or two year timeline. I think two to three years, we're going to start to be able to deliver that, and the country's going to start to be able to deliver that. And we're going to push to go as fast as possible. But it's, you know, the government is trying to provide some amount of demand signal. Industry is responding. I would say we are out ahead in terms of FPV drone manufacturers, just in terms of our actual proven production scale. And now with this facility, again, it's just a big empty box right now, but it will be the largest small drone factory in America. And yeah, it's going to allow us to, the way we think about it is it's going to allow us to create credible deterrents. I think under this scale, and without having the blueprint for factories like this, America does not have credible deterrents. And so that's what we are trying to build, to put it most simply.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: If there's not a blueprint that exists on how to spin up some sort of supply chain and factory like you're trying to do here, how are you kind of coming up with it?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: that, you know, from first principles. There are things to look out outside of drones. So, um, I've given this example a few times but of course the SpaceX Starlink user terminal factory is a really great example. I mean, the best example of modern consumer electronics manufacturing in America. Incredibly high rate, actually a lot of similarities between the products in terms of, you know, the like core processes that make up the product and, you know, being a very electronics and RF heavy system and then you have sort of this more simple structure that your main electronics go into and basically the way you build Starlink user terminals and the way you build drones are not that far away from each other and SpaceX has been able to do that at the, you know, millions per year and the full assembled product in that vertically integrated factory is cheaper than what they could do in China, which is unbelievable. And that's the bar, so...

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And that's like a first for America, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: It's a first for America in the last 10 years. We used to own a lot more manufacturing like that. And there's still some companies, you know, really big CMs that operate in America, but now they also, you know, they operate in a ton of countries and most of them have pretty strong ties into China now. And it was like America taught China how to do all the stuff that they're doing. Then China went and realized the advantage of that and made a concerted effort to be better at it and to scale it way beyond anybody else. So the things, like the limitations, you know, we run into is like electronics in America are so expensive and the lead time is much longer and the quality is much lower. And I believe SpaceX is the largest PCB manufacturer in America now. It's not one of the, you know, large board houses because and there aren't that many, it's SpaceX. I'm pretty sure. So that's sort of the blueprint. But a lot of what we need to do is look at China and understand how their drone factories are operating. A lot of this stuff is not wildly complex on its own, but building an entire factory that operates efficiently and then produces a high-performance system, that's where the hard, that's where the magic is. It's the, like, tying the performance of the product in every single little design detail that goes into that back into something that is manufacturable and then iterating, being able to iterate quickly while producing at high volume. So that's why it is such an advantage to own the factory and own the product is like there's this iteration loop that you do not get with

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: about that, and you need to have engineers who are responsible for, you know, their part being both performant and manufacturable and understanding the impacts to the customer when they change something, but also understanding the impacts to the line when they change something. So this is why at SpaceX, when an engineer is designing a part, they're owning everything all the way through manufacturing, and that's the same mindset that we take. It's, you know, not just like the responsible engineering mindset, but having people who own the entire life cycle of the of the part design.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And for the evolution of your own technology over the past, you know, couple of years, what have been the biggest step function improvements in capability?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: So we've spent a lot of time on radios and making jamming-resistant radios. So it's one of the number one things that you deal with in FPV drones on the battlefield is electronic warfare. So we've gone through a ton of different iterations on that. We've made the efficiency of our drone really second to none. Like every size category that we have a drone in, I've never seen a more efficient drone.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: How'd you do that?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: We're really good at flight performance, and we I think test in a very unique way. We're super analytical in our testing. We care a lot about the performance of the system. So there's a lot of drone companies and FPV companies that sort of either care about building components or care about building drones. They don't a lot of them don't really like build the most performant components to build the most performant drones. We do. And we try to make a very high performance integrated product because we own basically all the component design as well. And then there are, you know, we've heard very, very frequently from the actual end users that they really appreciate the way our drone flies, the amount of precision it gives them, and just how well it's built. We've had units in Ukraine tell us that this is the first actual like military spec drone they've ever used. And they've used, you know, all the FPVs. So that's pretty cool. And that's been a good bit of work to get there. And in the past, you know, we've definitely shipped products that are not up to like the spec that I want for a for a military system. And even on the current product, there's tons of stuff that needs to be improved, and we're working on we're working on new revisions of everything and a lot of new products right now. So over the next few months, there'll be a lot of really cool stuff coming out and being visible publicly. That's going to be pretty significant changes in the way our system can operate.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: excited about those as well. I can't talk about most of the biggest step changes that we've had because a lot of those have been recent and not announced yet, but I'll say that our sort of cohesiveness and performance of our entire system across the ground station to the drone and the way the operator interfaces with all that, um, it's going to get a really big level up soon.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: You're scaling up production, but you also have to basically be improving the product as fast as possible. What's going through your mind when you know something can be improved, but you also have to ship at the same time so you've got to make that trade-off?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's honestly, it can be such a frustrating feeling to even see, you know, we have products that are awesome. We have like prototypes of them that we are flying internally, like we're using every single day, but they're not production products. There's a huge difference between a prototype and a production product. And so there's this like, you have to, you know, it's a little frustrating to be like looking at this thing that's so beautiful that you want all of your customers to have, but it's like six months away even though it already exists. Um, because the time it takes to actually properly qualify something and move it into production, do your production validation builds, uh, and then actually start shipping it to the customer, there's a decent lead time there. Um, and yeah, so it's like we also try to measure how much we can show the new stuff to current customers because they get really excited and they want to buy it too quickly. And we're like, hold on, you know, this isn't a production product. Like if we try to ship this to you, we'll have a lot of problems. It will be, you know, not very efficient for us because we're doing these like small batches. So we kind of limit the exposure of the new stuff until it's really ready. Uh, but it's always a feeling of I wish this was, you know, I wish we could ship it right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Are you just basically like living I don't know, six months or 12 months in the future and then you're having to ship the 12 month, you know, 6-12 month old product simply because of that?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Most of the time, yes. Yeah, um, but this is, I mean, this is quite normal. I think it's just like learning it, as we build up the sort of suite of products in the company and improve our, the way we design products and the way we validate products and move them over to production. So there's a lot of like learning here, but, you know, many many companies deal with this. It's just kind of funny to see it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: One of the things that you're most focused on is you want to be like the first drone that is just fully made in America with all the components and like sub-assemblies and everything. Um, how close are you to that and like what are the key constraints to actually getting there?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I mean, if, it's just about how far down the supply chain you go. Um, at, there's just every level is hard work.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Do you want to talk about each of those levels?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, you know, we've gone to the biggest supply chain...

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: supply chain hurdle we've had to clear so far is getting all of the quote critical components China-free. Um, and then we've gone beyond that definition as well. So we, you know, we go down to things like motors as well. Um, and then also we've gone down to the neodymium that goes into the magnets that goes into our motors. Uh, but that's not work that we can always do directly. I mean most of it's not, right? It's actually just finding the right supplier and building a partnership with them, building a relationship with them.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: And so when I say it's like just how far down the supply chain you go, you know, Neurus is not going to be the company that can solve like neodymium mining and refining in America, at least not for a long time. Um,

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Not going to go full Tesla where you're like building the lithium plant?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Not yet. Um, we can't, you know, we we have to build high rate assembly and test, then we have to build high rate um, component manufacturing, then, you know, certain sub-components. But you start to get to the like, you know, chips and magnets and those things are obviously insanely hard. Um, you don't you don't get to do those until you're you just hugely scaled.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: So a lot of this stuff is a a team effort for for America and for allied countries. Um, to go back to your original question, you were quite far away from a a drone that is fully made in America because if you if you want to do that, you're talking about every microchip, every, you know, mineral. It's just like it's such an impossible list.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I actually don't think made in America is, you know, fully made in America is the most important step though. It's um, can we build drones with an allied supply chain, um, with certain like biases towards America or other regions depending on who the customer is. Um, but like, you know, could the allied world build a number of drones that is competitive with China and, you know, countries like Russia. That's what we care about more than anything.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: But then for each customer there is a a bias towards having, you know, very domestic supply chains and manufacturing, which is is understandable. So over time we'll figure out how to do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: One of the things that you talked about with me right before we started was this idea of there's part of it which is you want to be able to have, you know, there's building a million drones a year and then there's also the capability to build a million drones. And right now we just don't have the capability. So how are you thinking about basically making sure as quickly as possible we have that capability, even if you're not necessarily producing a million?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: That's why we're focusing really hard on the factory and and also thinking about if we are building a-

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: factory that's capable of peaking at a million drones per year what rate can it run at that is sustainable um which is much lower than a million per year and also you know what are the effective ways of of financing and selling capacity versus just drones so those are some of the questions that that exist um for that right like the we can't control the demand side and there is no program for a million drones per year yet and so we have to think about what is the most existential thing which is the capacity um and then and then build around that and we're very very willing to invest our own capital into that vision well before the government is is paying for a million drones

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: just like you did on the first factory

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: yeah but the you know the capex investment in the first factory's not that significant really like the team and the engineering costs are the biggest and and the inventory the working capital costs have been the biggest um have been the biggest factors so far the investment into component manufacturing and automated assembly and test at millions per year scale like that's where the um that's where it becomes a serious financial question but we're willing to take a good amount of that risk ourselves uh because we're fundamentally a mission driven company we know this thing needs to exist so it would be somewhat silly in my opinion to wait around for uh for the government to to say okay yeah we're gonna buy all those drones um you have to operate on some level of uncertainty

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: yeah this is a little bit more like uh the spacex situation with just figuring out launch cost to orbit and like mass to orbit and how do we just scale that as quickly as possible and it turns out you know at least in their case uh the best customer was just themselves for that mass to orbit right um and if you just listen to the government you wouldn't scale it

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: yeah absolutely um and and you know the government doesn't need to be our only customer for uh for forever it shouldn't be actually um but we have to start if we're trying to fix the drone industrial base we have to start in defense because right now or you know in defense is where the regulatory environment and the customer exists that just totally excludes the chinese competitors uh because it's not possible to go head to head with the chinese competitors in the uh you know consumer enterprise market right now but that's shifting um and it's shifting in america especially with the new ruling by the fcc on dji and just the the direction that we are the country is pushing it um it's clear

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I think that we're going to have to have a—and really should have—a domestic drone industrial base that supports all of the applications, not just defense. And that's where the millions-per-year volume is also just extremely reasonable. You know, if we're talking about millions per year just for defense, you know, that means we're probably doing some significant stockpiling, we're using a lot in training. I think we should be doing both of those things. But if you look at the number of drones that DJI sells in America every year, you know, millions makes a ton of sense. So we just have to build a, you know, competitor to that. And the government seems to be pushing in the direction of just not allowing DJI to operate here long term, which is the right decision. So that creates opportunity for actually fixing an industrial base, not just building sort of a, you know, one-off factory that then just sits unutilized.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: If you had to like look at what the state of the current drone environment is, I think China produces something on the order of like 70 million drones a year, whereas the US produces less than like a couple hundred thousand. How do we actually bridge that gap to a point where we're even semi-on-parity?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I mean, I think, you know, everything we've been talking about so far is somewhat the answer to that. It's, you know, I believe it's a very vertically integrated factory, and then many of them. It's basically our roadmap. Like that's what—that's what the answer should be, because that's what the goal of the company is. So we'll start talking about some of this stuff even more publicly in the coming months, but I think it's, yeah, the investment into the factory capability and and finding the use cases over time that consume at high volume.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Going through the first like production ramp in your old factory, what was kind of the biggest moments of production hell? And then, you know, in this new one, what do you kind of predicting the biggest hurdles are going to be?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: The biggest moments of production hell so far have actually been supply chain hell, where you, you know, we order 98% of the parts you need to build the drone, and then you realize, hey, somehow the $5 part didn't get ordered and it has a six-week lead time. So you're sitting there with, you know, basically the entire BOM cost of the drone for thousands of drones, and you can't build them because you don't have a $5 part.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: And you have limited space in your factory, so it's just piling up.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: It's piling up, but it's just like you can't operate a factory that's not—or you can't—you can't sustain a factory that's not producing. It's just like that's how you—that's how you ruin your ramp. So those have been some of the hardest moments that we dealt with in 2025. We—that didn't happen to us a lot, but there were certain times where supply chain really limited the output.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Amount of drones that could go out the door and drones that could be built and go out the door. Um, we've tried to spread out sort of the ramp of assembly capacity reasonably so it's not like these huge jumps. It's like a pretty even buildup and that helps a lot. You know, we can sort of like staff more and more over time. We are consistently driving tact times down across the line. Um, and and having a smooth ramp is just just makes everything so much easier. Um, this is also why we do want to be sort of ahead in capacity from where we think the contracts are going to be because the worst the way you really get into trouble is by signing up for a contract that you don't already have the capacity for on a timeline that is too quick.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Impossible to meet.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and that sounds obvious, but there's a lot of companies that do this. Actually, I'd say the majority of defense companies do this or like new defense startups do this is is like kind of speak in the manner, like say you have the capacity but have not actually proven it, get a contract, have a delivery window, try to go through the production ramp, realize that it's actually really hard, um, and then fail to deliver on time. That's like the classic story. And I just wish that people would speak a little bit more honestly about the the production capacity they hold today, like what they've actually proven because, um, you know, the point I was making here is like, yeah, we've got a big empty box that in a few years is going to hopefully produce a million drones per year. Um, but the the space isn't everything, the equipment isn't everything, the team is everything, it's the whole combined thing working together correctly, actually producing the stuff. That's how you measure capacity. Um, and a lot of people don't don't understand that and get themselves into trouble. Um, and then that leaves a bad taste in the mouth of the customer. So for us, it's figuring out how we can while still being sort of financially smart, build capacity ahead of demand, um, and try to match those two things closely together.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: How successful have you been like to date on when you say you have a timeline that can work, that it actually does, and then if things do go wrong, how do you kind of rapidly course correct that?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: That's a good question. Um, because we've not been perfect. Um, you know, there have absolutely been failures in in our history of thinking we could do something faster and making a commitment and then, you know, realizing we can't. I think we've done a very good job of maintaining high communication with the customer, like, you know, balancing understanding what is actually mission critical for them versus, you know, what is their sort of desire versus, you know, like a lot of it is about the relationship and communication with the customer. Um...

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I've seen that go really wrong for other companies, just making these commitments, burning bridges. We try really hard not to do that. So like being, yeah, being very honest and then just getting better, building the muscle of, you know, engineering and production timelining. We started with a team that had, you know, great experience from other verticals and, and you know, have done parts of this before, but it's clearly a learning process doing it for the first time as Neros. And so we've, we've, I'd say, gotten much better at it. And there, there was a time like early 2025, you know, we're trying to ship our ground station for the first time, or ship like production units of it for the first time. And that was really biting us, just getting everything functional and reliable and sort of, you know, working the way we had promised to people. And that was one of the hardest pushes of like, we just didn't give ourselves enough time to finish the engineering and then hand it off to production. So you know, you learn by making mistakes. Uh, but I think the severity of those mistakes are seriously defined by like how well you communicate externally and then make it up to people, or, or you know, figure out like what can you do for them, uh, your customer typically, that is solving some of the issue even if you're not ready to solve all of the issue.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse
full_transcript.text: Elon has this idea where I think in the past he had timeline slips a lot more than he does today. And now he's got some idea where he's trying to do roughly like 50% of the time, he wants the maximally aggressive timeline, that 50% of the time they can actually hit it. And that by definition means that half the time they won't. Um, but without that, there's not the same level of like urgency. Um, how do you kind of think about maintaining just extreme urgency while also making sure that you're not like committing things that are impossible?

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: I think maintaining urgency comes a lot from the mission and the sort of like broader timeline that the company is thinking on. And that's why we're, you know, right now as a 120-person, you know, tens of thousands of drones per year company talking about a million drones per year. Um, in some ways that's ridiculous. full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: And rocket launches are a great thing to drive urgency, right? You have a launch date and it's very binary if it succeeds or fails. Um, the soldiers, they knew our frequencies we were operating on. They'd we'd already done a bunch of testing runs and they'd collected data. So this was not a realistic scenario. full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: super high end of the spectrum, uh, you know, we like flew over Venezuela and turned off all the lights, right? That's insane. full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: that they had this system and capability now, which just could totally change the way they were thinking about fighting in an area like that.

full_transcript.text: In 2025 we sent over 6,000 drones to Ukraine. Um, so that was... from that we've gotten to work with a lot more units. Um, and, you know, Neros does not build radios for all of these frequencies. So, uh, you know, we- we actually help the units with supplying and- and swapping out new radios into the drones, um, and- and things of that nature to make the platform as effective as possible for the specific user. Um, we measure our success very heavily off of battlefield results and like true effectiveness of the product. And you know we see this with all of- we try to track this with all of our customers, not just in Ukraine, but it's- it's absolutely vital that we are measuring ourselves by like the product making a real difference in the world versus, you know, just the sort of like top level size of the contracts or, you know, whatever. Because there's- there's a lot of metrics you can measure yourself by that are sort of removed from reality and don't actually mean you're being super impactful. Like you can build a very large company in the defense space without having a seriously measurable impact. Um, so that's something we care a lot about and our operations in Ukraine, um, are- are part of that. Um, and yeah, we've gotten- as I said we've gotten to deploy a lot more systems there and establish those relationships a lot better and- and build a better system for feedback too. full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Sure, but you have to prioritize stuff, you can't do everything at once. And you also have to listen to the problem, not the proposed solution for a lot of stuff, right? Like what you want to do is deeply understand the problems that the end users are running into, and especially in Ukraine, they have a lot of amazing knowledge on how to build good drones, so there are learnings to take away from that, but there's also cases where we do things differently and we, you know, they, for an example, our propellers are much more like flexible than the standard prop that they use in Ukraine, or the standard propellers that they use in Ukraine. So a lot of units will receive the drones and just like see and feel the prop and be like 'this will not work well with a heavy payload because the prop is too flexible, it will deflect under the load.' And every time we see that, we make a bet with them, we're like, 'you know, go try it, go actually fly it and compare it back to back with your props.' And then they do that and they're like, 'okay, yeah, it works really well.' But it's just this like knowledge that they've built up is like stiffer props work better with heavy payloads, and that's a very logical thing to think, and generally the props that are designed for heavy payloads are much stiffer, and so like completely understandable why that's an assumption. You can, you know, we broke that assumption by testing a lot of different stuff and ending up on this prop. And so yeah, we just like have to have that good back and forth with the customer.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: At first undervalued, like you tell someone your drone's better than yours because it flies better and they're like, I don't care, I like how much does it carry, how far does it go, you know, does it hit the target? Like go try it. Um, see how it flies with a heavier payload, um, show that it actually works better. Um, and you know, we've again, we got to that answer by doing an insane amount of testing, uh, and we we are very confident in the fact that it is a better solution. Um, and so you just sort of have to break through assumptions sometimes.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson

You do a lot of testing in development and then you also do a lot of testing in qualification um to ensure that the product is, you know, meeting or exceeding the requirements that you've set and will be reliable. And then there's testing in manufacturing, so there's component-level testing along the line to make sure that every component before it gets built into a drone is actually functional and then end-of-line testing uh to to see if the whole system is uh is uh meeting spec. So again, yeah, wide category, we've gotten better at all of it, um, but I was sort of talking about the like performance, the flight testing in development. Um, we I think have good instinct there given our racing background, but uh it's also an engineering task. Um, so our uh our the person who leads our flight test team, uh, Levi, he's an old friend of mine from drone racing. Um, he's one of the only people at the company who's younger than me. Um, and he he runs our flight testing team and does an incredible job at it. Um, but I've also get I've gotten to see him grow in that role by learning from working with amazing engineers to understand like what is the data output they need from me.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: flight test to make the product better because a drone racer you hand them a drone and you say like all right how does this fly or like go fly it and tell me what you think like yeah man it's locked in it flies on rails like there's just like these terms that are completely useless to engineering an engineer does not want to hear the drone is you know locked in or not locked in so you know we built a team that's much better at giving you know analytical feedback and stuff that's really helpful to engineers and we test every single day so we operate out in the desert and so our team is able to go from our headquarters to the desert every day and they actually can stay out in the desert and so we just like run I mean we're usually testing like three four five different things at once and doing it every single day and maximizing the number of sets and reps

full_transcript.text: so I'd say fiber optic has been one of the bigger development step changes in FPV over the last two years in many ways like you look at an FPV drone from two years ago to one today a lot of them look really similar there's small differences there's things that are tweaked drones are generally getting a little bit bigger you know there's shifted frequencies but a lot of the technology's also stayed the same fiber's been one of those sort of actual significant shifts so what it enables is operation when you would not be able to sustain a radio link so that's either in a highly contested jammed environment or beyond radio line of sight or even you know in a tunnel or something like that so it's still you know it's not it's been a huge factor in Ukraine but it's not like the majority of missions are being flown with fiber fiber's really finicky use and much more expensive and it reduces your payload and you need bigger drones there's a lot of drawbacks but we saw how important it is as one of the tools in the tool belt one of the pieces of the complete FPV ecosystem and what we've been building is a complete FPV ecosystem so to do that we need to have a fiber optic solution and just like everything else there was no alternative or no option that doesn't have Chinese components so we started working on it and then we actually found a partner on it to help move quickly and so we've you know now validated Archer fiber it's going into production shortly and will be you know very important it will not be the majority of drones that we ship but it will be an important percentage of drones that we ship

So, I- I fully believe that the reason why FPV drones have been so effective is because they give a human operator the most precise control of an object through 3D space at a large distance. Um, there isn't really anything else, especially in the- in the cost category where you can be sitting 20, 30 kilometers away and precisely navigating, you know, like- inch-level precision basically. Um, it's a- it's a wild technology, it's- it's like, um, you know, if- if you'd thought about it 50 years ago, it's like a sci-fi capability, you know, it's basically Ender's Game. So-

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: But I think the- the importance of that actually sometimes gets overlooked in the, uh, in the rhetoric about autonomous drone systems. Um, everyone's thinking about the drone swarm, which will be important, but you can't- you can't overlook giving- why it's so important to give the operator ultimate control.

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson
full_transcript.text: Right now, there's no AI model that is better than a human pilot in- in basically every circumstance and dealing with the edge cases that are, you know, all across the battlefield. Um, you're basically saying, like, here's the general area for the target, go find it, do, um, object recognition and then strike it autonomously. Problem with that is basically everything on the battlefield now is highly camouflaged and highly dynamic. So all of these object detection algorithms fail pretty miserably unless it's like a tank driving down the road out in the open.

full_transcript.speaker: Ti Morse

full_transcript.speaker: Soren Monroe-Anderson

full_transcript.text: um as we move forward we're thinking about how do we give leverage to that person how do we make it so they can effectively control multiple systems at once how can they for the targets where you don't need to manually pilot it into the exact spot um just say you know click click here go there um that is all stuff that we're working on so I think the future is going to look like each warfighter able to do more and the barrier for entry being much lower um so part of the problem with FPV systems is that they're quite hard to use um there's a lot of nuance in using them so you compare like a DJI drone versus an FPV drone basically anyone can fly a DJI drone out of the box and it just works you don't have to do anything to it and then you move over to an FPV system it's like

highlights: Neuros successfully defeated every U.S. military jammer they encountered during testing, at one point pranking soldiers by hovering a drone carrying a jar of Smucker’s jam just 15 feet from active interference. This performance gap highlights a critical vulnerability in current electronic warfare systems when faced with modern, high-performance FPV technology.

The company is rapidly scaling from a small garage to a 250,000-square-foot facility with the goal of becoming the first American manufacturer to produce one million drones per year. This massive expansion is intended to create "credible deterrence" by establishing sovereign manufacturing capacity that can match the production scales of adversaries like China.

Following the SpaceX blueprint, Neuros is pursuing deep vertical integration to overcome the high costs and long lead times of the domestic electronics supply chain. By owning the entire manufacturing process—from component design to final assembly—they aim to build high-performance systems that are cost-competitive with Chinese alternatives.

While many defense startups focus on fully autonomous swarms, Neuros prioritizes "human-in-the-loop" precision because current AI models frequently fail to identify camouflaged or dynamic targets in real-world combat. The company believes the ultimate battlefield advantage lies in giving operators the most precise control over an object through 3D space at long distances.

Rebuilding the American industrial base is viewed as an existential necessity, as the U.S. currently lacks the high-quantity, low-cost production capabilities required for modern warfare. The CEO emphasizes that a strong domestic manufacturing engine is the only way to ensure national security and remain competitive over the next decade.
summary:
Nearos, led by CEO Soren Monroe-Anderson, is rapidly scaling from a small garage operation to a 250,000-square-foot facility with the ambitious goal of producing one million FPV drones annually. Drawing inspiration from SpaceX’s vertically integrated manufacturing model, the company aims to establish a sovereign American industrial base that bypasses reliance on Chinese components and lead times. This expansion is a direct response to a massive shift in Pentagon priorities, which now views small, high-performance drones as a critical military competency following their proven effectiveness in the Russia-Ukraine conflict.

Technically, Nearos distinguishes itself through extreme flight precision and electronic warfare resilience, famously demonstrated by a prank where they hovered a drone carrying a jar of Smucker’s jam in front of military jammers that failed to disable it. While the company is developing advanced solutions like fiber-optic drones to bypass radio interference, Monroe-Anderson emphasizes that "human-in-the-loop" FPV control remains superior to current AI for navigating dynamic, camouflaged battlefields. He argues that the immediate priority is building "credible deterrence" by ensuring America has the physical capacity to out-produce adversaries in a high-intensity conflict.

The transcript also explores the leadership and philosophical challenges of building a defense startup. Monroe-Anderson discusses the necessity of intense "fly-off" sprints to win contracts and the difficulty of transitioning from a hands-on builder to a CEO focused on recruitment and long-term strategy. Ultimately, he frames the restoration of American domestic manufacturing as an existential requirement, arguing that a strong industrial base is a moral necessity for preserving democratic values and global stability in the face of rising peer adversaries like China.
